summary: Late Roman / early medieval philosopher (6th century), known primarily as the author of The Consolation of Philosophy, written while imprisoned by Theodoric. Also important as the translator of Aristotle and Porphyry into Latin, providing the main access to Greek philosophy for medieval Europe.

works.name: The Consolation of Philosophy
works.description: Boethius's most famous work, written while he was imprisoned and awaiting execution by Theodoric the Great. It alternates between prose and poetry (Menippean satire structure) and centers on a conversation between a fictional Boethius and the allegorical Lady Philosophy, who wears a garment with pi at the bottom and theta at the top connected by a staircase. The work popularized the image of the Wheel of Fortune and addresses free will, divine providence, and the nature of happiness.

clues.clue: Written while imprisoned by Theodoric; centers on conversation with Lady Philosophy
examples: this philosopher is told about Providence and the 'wheel of fortune' by a Lady who personifies the title discipline in a work that he wrote while imprisoned by Theodoric
this medieval thinker who popularized the image of the 'wheel of fortune' in his Consolation of Philosophy

this medieval thinker who popularized the image of the 'wheel of fortune'

clues.clue: Includes a proof of God based on the standard of perfect happiness; muses interrupted by Lady Philosophy at the beginning
examples: This man outlines a proof of God based on the standard of perfect happiness in a book that begins with the muses being interrupted by the title allegorical lady.

clues.clue: Paradox of the good having power while the bad are powerless
examples: This thinker's most famous work contains a discussion on the paradox surrounding how the good have power, while the bad are powerless.

this thinker, who was imprisoned and killed by Theodoric the Great

works.name: Translations of Aristotle and Porphyry
works.description: Boethius's translations of Aristotle and Porphyry's Isagoge were the primary means by which medieval Europe accessed Greek philosophy until the 12th century. His translation of the Isagoge became a standard medieval logic textbook and codified the 'problem of universals.' He also translated Aristotle's On Interpretation and other logical works.

clues.clue: His translations of Aristotle were the only works of Aristotle available to medieval Europeans until the 12th century
examples: Until the 12th century, the only works of Aristotle available to medieval Europeans were translations by this philosopher
this scholar whose translations of Aristotle provided almost all of the Aristotelian corpus available to medieval thinkers

clues.clue: Argued that written words signify spoken words, which signify thoughts, which denote things ('order of speaking')
examples: this philosopher argued that written words always signify spoken words, which themselves signify thoughts, which themselves denote things, a theory he termed the 'order of speaking.'

clues.clue: Duns Scotus was one of the last major figures to write a commentary on Boethius's Isagoge
examples: One of the last major figures to write a commentary on Boethius' version of the Isagoge was this British philosopher

works.name: De Musica and Music Theory
works.description: Boethius's De institutione musica was the most influential source for Greek-derived musical thought in the medieval era. He divided music into three kinds: musica mundana (celestial harmony), musica humana (bodily harmony), and instrumental music. The work popularized Pythagorean ratio-based derivations of consonances.

clues.clue: De institutione musica divided music into musica mundana, musica humana, and instrumental music
examples: This music theorist divided music into three kinds: musica mundana that holds the celestial objects in harmony, musica humana that regulates our bodies, and lastly the actual instrumental music we hear.
The first section of this late antiquity thinker's De institutione musica discusses the music of the spheres

clues.clue: Most influential source for Greek-derived musical thought in the medieval era
examples: whose 6th-century treatise Fundamentals of Music or De institutione musica, was the single most influential source for Greek-derived musical thought in the medieval era

clues.clue: Popularized Pythagorean ratio-based derivation of consonances (4:3 for perfect fourth)
examples: Boethius' treatise popularized Pythagoras' ratio-driven derivation of the perfect consonances

works.name: De Topicis Differentiis and Logic
works.description: Boethius wrote several important works on logic, including De Topicis Differentiis, which defines topics as 'parts of an argument that give the speaker confidence' and juxtaposes Cicero's and Themistius's treatments. He also wrote De Divisione and the Opuscula Sacra.

clues.clue: De Topicis Differentiis defines topics as 'parts of an argument that give confidence' and compares Cicero and Themistius
examples: The major work of logic by this author defines the title entities as 'parts of an argument that give the speaker and his audience confidence' and juxtaposes Cicero's and Themistius's treatment

clues.clue: Concept of partition illustrated in Descriptio with a man carrying a bag of human limbs
examples: Descriptio includes a man carrying a bag of human limbs to illustrate this thinker's concept of partition.

clues.clue: Emphasized supremacy of logic in Overview of the Study of Rhetoric
examples: Following Aristotle, this Latin writer emphasized the supremacy of logic in his Overview of the Study of Rhetoric.

comprehensive_summary:
Boethius (Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius) is a 6th-century Roman philosopher most famous for The Consolation of Philosophy, written while he was imprisoned and awaiting execution by Theodoric the Great. The work is structured as an alternation between prose and poetry, and Joel Relihan has argued it follows the Menippean satire form. It centers on a dialogue between a fictional Boethius and the allegorical Lady Philosophy, who appears wearing a garment embroidered with the Greek letters pi at the bottom and theta at the top, connected by a staircase. Lady Philosophy consoles Boethius by discussing providence, fate, and the nature of happiness.

The Consolation popularized the image of the Wheel of Fortune, with Lady Philosophy describing her 'unchanging sport' of turning the highest to the bottom and the lowest to the top. The work compares God to a ship's 'rudder and helm' and draws on Plato's Gorgias to argue that evildoers punish themselves by ceasing to exist as truly human. Book V addresses the compatibility of divine prescience with free will, comparing God's timeless perspective to a point at the center of a circle equidistant from the entire circumference. The work was later translated into English by Geoffrey Chaucer. Fortuna from the Consolation also plays a major role in the medieval manuscript Carmina Burana, famously set by Carl Orff.

Beyond the Consolation, Boethius was immensely important as a transmitter of Greek thought to the Latin West. His translations of Aristotle were virtually the only Aristotelian works available to medieval Europeans until the 12th century. His translation of Porphyry's Isagoge became the standard medieval logic textbook and codified the 'problem of universals,' which would dominate medieval philosophy. Lorenzo Valla dubbed him 'the first of the Scholastics.'

Boethius also made significant contributions to music theory through his De institutione musica, which divided music into three categories: musica mundana (the harmony of the celestial spheres), musica humana (the harmony of the human body), and instrumental music. This was the most influential source for Greek-derived musical thought throughout the medieval era. His logical works include De Topicis Differentiis, which compares the approaches of Cicero and Themistius to topics, and De Divisione. A Christian legend held that he married the hymnist Helpes, though Edward Gibbon disputed this.
